learning_objectives: Distinguish organizational change management from project change control
Compare Kotter, Lewin, and ADKAR change models

lecture_notes.introduction: Change management is not about managing scope changes. It is about helping people transition from current behaviors to new ones so the project's benefits are realized.
key_concepts: Change management vs. change control: people adoption vs. scope governance
Lewin's model: Unfreeze-Change-Refreeze
Kotter's 8 steps: urgency, coalition, vision, communication, empowerment, quick wins, consolidation, anchoring
ADKAR: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement
Change readiness assessment: organizational capacity, willingness, and history of change
lecture_notes.demonstration: Assessing change readiness for a hospital transitioning from paper records to an electronic health records system

review.value: Sequential model: you cannot create Desire without first creating Awareness; each step enables the next
Organizations with history of failed change have lower readiness; past failures create skepticism and resistance

learning_objectives: Develop a change management strategy aligned with project objectives
Identify impacted groups and assess the degree of change for each
Design a sponsorship model for change initiatives
readings: Prosci, Best Practices in Change Management (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: A change management strategy is the roadmap for the people side of change. It must be as deliberate as the project plan.
key_concepts: Impact assessment: who is affected, how much change they face, and what they lose/gain
Change magnitude: incremental adjustment vs. transformational change
Sponsorship model: active and visible executive sponsorship at every level
Coalition of sponsors: primary sponsor, mid-level sponsors, frontline supervisors
Change management plan: communication, training, coaching, resistance management, reinforcement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a stakeholder impact assessment for a company-wide ERP implementation

review.value: Effective sponsors communicate directly, participate visibly, and hold people accountable; passive sponsorship is a leading cause of change failure
Maps each group's current state to future state, identifies what changes for them specifically, and rates the magnitude of change

learning_objectives: Design a change communication plan targeting awareness and desire
Develop training programs that build knowledge and ability

lecture_notes.introduction: Communication answers 'why change?' and 'what's in it for me?' Training answers 'how do I do this?'
key_concepts: Communication for Awareness: what is changing, why, and what happens if we don't change
Communication for Desire: what's in it for the individual, addressing the WIIFM question
Preferred senders: employees want to hear 'why' from senior leaders and 'how it affects me' from their direct manager
Training design: just-in-time delivery, role-specific content, hands-on practice, job aids
Effectiveness measurement: surveys, knowledge tests, observation of new behaviors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a multi-channel communication plan for a new time-tracking system rollout

review.value: Prosci research: employees want business reasons from executives and personal impact from their direct supervisor; reversing this reduces effectiveness
Training delivered immediately before the change is more effective than training weeks in advance, because skills decay without practice

learning_objectives: Diagnose resistance using the ADKAR model
Apply coaching techniques to move individuals through change
Manage persistent resistance without creating adversarial dynamics

lecture_notes.introduction: Resistance is not the enemy. It is information about where the change process is failing to address people's needs.
key_concepts: ADKAR barrier diagnosis: resistance at Awareness means they don't know; at Desire means they don't want to; at Knowledge means they don't know how
Manager as change coach: direct supervisors are the most influential change agents
Coaching conversations: listen, acknowledge, explore, recommend, follow up
Persistent resistance: after coaching, some resistance requires consequence management
Bridges' transition model: ending (letting go), neutral zone (uncertainty), new beginning (embracing change)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-playing coaching conversations with employees at different ADKAR barrier points

review.value: The first barrier point determines the intervention: you cannot train someone (Knowledge) who doesn't want to change (Desire)
Bridges: the most dangerous phase; old ways abandoned but new ways not yet comfortable; productivity drops; provide extra support

class_schedule.topic: Reinforcement, Sustainability, and Measuring Change Success
learning_objectives: Design reinforcement mechanisms to sustain change adoption
Measure change management effectiveness using leading and lagging indicators
Integrate change management into the project management methodology
readings: PMI Managing Change in Organizations, Ch. 7-8

lecture_notes.introduction: Without reinforcement, people revert to old behaviors. Sustainable change requires mechanisms that outlast the project team.
key_concepts: Reinforcement mechanisms: performance metrics, recognition, accountability, compliance checks
Corrective reinforcement: addressing early signs of reversion quickly
Celebration: recognizing milestones and early adopters to build momentum
Change success metrics: adoption rate, utilization rate, proficiency, speed of adoption
Integrating CM with PM: change management as a workstream within the project plan, not an afterthought
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a reinforcement plan for a post-go-live ERP system with adoption metrics dashboard

review.value: The 'R' in ADKAR; without it, individuals backslide; direct manager reinforcement is most effective
Change management activities should be embedded in the WBS with resources, timelines, and deliverables, not treated as a separate stream

class_schedule.topic: Change Management for Digital Transformation and Agile
learning_objectives: Apply change management in agile and iterative delivery contexts
Manage change fatigue in continuous transformation environments
Build organizational change capability as a competitive advantage

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital transformation requires continuous change, not one-time transitions. Organizations must build change capability as a core competence.
key_concepts: Agile change management: iterative deployment means iterative change management
Change fatigue: too many simultaneous changes overwhelm the organization
Change saturation assessment: how much change capacity does the organization have?
Change capability maturity: ad hoc, project-level, organizational, embedded
Kotter's dual operating system: combining hierarchy (stability) with network (agility)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Assessing change saturation for an organization undergoing three simultaneous transformation initiatives

review.value: Symptoms: passive compliance, cynicism, decreased productivity, voluntary turnover; managed by pacing and prioritizing changes
Organizations that build change management into their culture change faster and more successfully; it becomes a competitive advantage

questions.question: An employee says: 'I understand why the company is implementing the new CRM system, but I don't see how it helps me. My current spreadsheet works fine.' At which ADKAR stage is this person blocked, and what specific intervention should their manager use?
questions.answer: The employee is blocked at the Desire stage. They have Awareness (they understand the why) but lack Desire (they don't want to change because they don't see personal benefit). Intervention: the direct manager should have a one-on-one conversation addressing WIIFM (What's In It For Me). Specifically: show how the CRM eliminates their manual data entry pain points, demonstrate time savings they'll gain, explain how CRM data visibility could support their promotion case, address their concern about the spreadsheet by acknowledging it works but showing what they're missing. The manager should NOT send them to training (Knowledge) because training without Desire leads to compliance, not adoption.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Desire as the barrier
Explains why it's Desire not Knowledge

questions.question: Compare Kotter's 8-step model and Prosci's ADKAR model. In what situation would you use each, and can they be used together?
questions.answer: Kotter's 8-step model is an organizational-level framework focused on leading the overall change initiative: creating urgency, building coalitions, communicating vision, and anchoring changes in culture. It is best for guiding the organizational strategy and leadership actions. ADKAR is an individual-level framework focused on moving each person through the change: building awareness, desire, knowledge, ability, and reinforcement. It is best for diagnosing individual resistance and designing targeted interventions. They can and should be used together: Kotter provides the organizational roadmap (macro), while ADKAR provides the individual change framework (micro). For example, Kotter's 'communicate the vision' step creates ADKAR's Awareness; Kotter's 'empower action' enables ADKAR's Ability.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes organizational vs. individual focus
Explains how they complement each other

questions.question: A department of 30 people has adopted a new project management tool. After 3 months, usage data shows 20 people use it daily, 7 use it occasionally, and 3 never use it. Describe your reinforcement strategy for each group.
questions.answer: Daily users (20): Recognize and celebrate them publicly. Ask 2-3 to become peer champions who help others. Gather their feedback for improvements. Ensure they have access to advanced features and training. Occasional users (7): Diagnose their ADKAR barrier (likely Ability or Desire). Provide refresher training focused on their specific workflows. Have their manager set clear expectations for daily use and check in weekly. Pair each with a daily user as a buddy. Non-users (3): Have their direct manager conduct a one-on-one coaching conversation to understand the specific barrier. If Desire, address WIIFM. If Ability, provide personalized training. If persistent resistance after coaching and support, escalate to performance management. Never punish first; always diagnose and coach first.

rubric.key: Appropriate strategy for daily users (recognition, champions)
Appropriate strategy for occasional users (diagnosis, support)
Appropriate strategy for non-users (coaching before consequences)

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'change saturation' and describe two symptoms that indicate an organization has exceeded its change capacity.
questions.answer: Change saturation occurs when the volume and pace of change exceeds the organization's capacity to absorb it. Just as a sponge can only absorb so much water, people and organizations have finite capacity for change. Two symptoms: (1) Passive compliance: employees go through the motions of adopting changes but do not truly internalize them, leading to superficial adoption that reverts when monitoring stops. 'Check-the-box' behavior replaces genuine engagement. (2) Increased voluntary turnover: high-performing employees who have options leave because the constant change creates stress and uncertainty. They seek stability elsewhere. Exit interviews reveal 'too much change too fast' as a departure reason.

rubric.key: Explains the mechanism behind each symptom

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Change Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete change management plan for a significant organizational change initiative, including impact assessment, ADKAR analysis, communication plan, training plan, resistance management plan, and reinforcement strategy.
objectives: Conduct stakeholder impact assessment for all affected groups
Perform ADKAR analysis for each stakeholder group
Design a multi-channel communication plan targeting awareness and desire
Develop a training plan targeting knowledge and ability
Create a resistance management and reinforcement strategy
deliverables: Stakeholder impact assessment matrix with change magnitude ratings
ADKAR analysis for each stakeholder group with gap identification
Communication plan with messages, channels, senders, and timeline (3-4 pages)
Training plan with role-specific curricula and delivery methods (2-3 pages)
Resistance management and reinforcement plan (2-3 pages)

criteria: All affected groups identified with change magnitude rated
Current state and future state described for each group
What changes specifically for each group is clear
ADKAR assessed for each group with barrier points identified
Analysis demonstrates understanding of the model
Messages address both Awareness and Desire
Correct senders assigned per Prosci research
Multiple channels used with appropriate timing
Reinforcement mechanisms are specific and sustainable
Resistance management approach is empathetic and structured

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on change models, strategy, resistance, and reinforcement

midterm_examination.description: Change models, impact assessment, and communication planning

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all change management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in coaching role-plays, case studies, and change readiness exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for coaching and role-play exercises. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Coaching role-plays are collaborative. The change management plan is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite Prosci, Kotter, and other change management frameworks.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized software required. Word processor, spreadsheet, and presentation tools sufficient.
